figures.caption: Fig. 1: A Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) policy navigating a procedurally generated Wave Function Collapse (WFC) warehouse layout in MuRoSim. The robot follows the A∗ subgoal path (orange) toward the goal (red) while reacting to LiDAR observations (green); the trajectory tail (blue) shows the executed motion.

Fig. 4: Trajectories of the four DRL configurations and the Carrot controller (at 1.0 m/s) on a maze (left) and a WFC obstacle map (right); markers as in Fig. 3. The baselines struggle on the maze: FF (blue) times out and GRU (orange) only escapes after extensive exploration, while the subgoal policies and Carrot track the A∗ path cleanly. On the WFC map, the subgoal policies and Carrot follow a near-optimal path, but Carrot collides at the goal because its fixed-speed tracker cannot decelerate before the final obstacle.

Fig. 5: Sim-to-real runs on the RoboMaster.1 (a) Third-person view of the cluttered open arena used for the combined policy. (b) RViz snapshot of the maze-like environment used to compare the sparse, combined, and GRU policies, showing the slam_toolbox map (gray), the live RPLidar scan (red), and the operator-set goal pose (green).

Fig. 2: Example environments from the four procedural map generators. (a) Sparse: randomly placed obstacles of varying shape and size. (b) Maze: corridor-based layout generated via Prim’s algorithm with wall removal. (c) Graph: passage network from randomly connected seed points. (d) WFC: tile-based layout using Wave Function Collapse with the four different presets.

Fig. 3: Trajectories of the five cross-evaluation policies on one representative map per environment type (left to right: sparse, maze, WFC obstacle, WFC warehouse). Colored lines show policy trajectories; end markers indicate outcome (⋆ success, × collision, ■ timeout); the green ring marks the goal radius. The combined policy succeeds on all four maps, while each specialist fails on at least one out-of-distribution layout: the sparse specialist collides in cluttered maze and warehouse environments, the maze specialist’s corridor-tuned speed produces visible orbital loops around the goal, and the graph specialist collides in both WFC layouts.
